_note: Consolidated real results for all judge variants — for an interactive comparison UI. See docs/FINDINGS.md for the written analysis.
test_set: llmbar_adversarial_100.jsonl — 100 items (25 each: GPTInst/GPTOut/manual/neighbor), order-swap on
training_means: updating the judge's CONTEXT (policy text + in-context examples), not model weights

variants.label: baseline-vanilla
variants.method: Standard LLM-as-judge: one call, generic 'which is better?' prompt, no rubric.

variants.label: structured-rubric
variants.method: Hand-engineered policy: derive criteria, judge correctness independently of fluency, explicit bias guards.
variants.note: +4.5 on adversarial, but the SAME rubric regressed -9 on easy/safety (RewardBench).

variants.label: continual self-critique
variants.method: Streams dev examples; every 3, reflects on its own errors vs the label and appends task-general lessons to its policy.
variants.note: Only 2 updates fired (sparse errors). Net distinct-item gain +2; biggest win is order-consistency 90->98%.

variants.label: teacher-driven (cross-family)
variants.method: Gemini judge; a GPT teacher (shown the answer key) diagnoses each error and writes a lesson + a few-shot example. Both grow the judge's context every 3 examples.
variants.note: Peaked at 88.5% (best of any variant) then drifted to 86.5%. Teacher cost negligible ($0.0005); $3.30 was the curve checkpoints. Early-stopping -> 88.5%.

promising: Cross-family grounded teaching (V5) beats self-critique (V2) — a different model family, shown the answer key, gives better lessons than self-reflection. Peak 88.5%.
A better starting policy (V1) helps (+4.5) cheaply.
Continual learning improves robustness (position-consistency) more than raw accuracy.
The learning curve reveals dynamics a before/after number hides (peak-then-drift).
limited: Hand-engineered rubrics are context-dependent — V1 helped on adversarial but regressed on easy/safety.
More continual learning over-corrects (drift): keep best-on-validation, not the final policy.
Sparse-error bottleneck: a strong base judge makes few mistakes, so only 2-3 updates fire — the ceiling is the data's hard errors, not the method.
Objective benchmarks (JudgeBench 97%) are saturated — only adversarial style has headroom.
